content:
Hi UX — writer here. Your brief asked what I'll write. Here's what shapes my decisions:

For recipe titles: I'll keep them SHORT — 2–4 words when possible (e.g. "Fluffy Pancakes", "Grilled Salmon", "Chocolate Cake"). Clear over clever.

For descriptions: I'll stick to 1–2 sentences max. Warm, practical voice — why you'd want to make it, no marketing fluff.

Two questions for you:
1. Is the card grid dense (compact spacing, descriptions clipped) or roomy (more breathing room, descriptions might run 2 full lines)? That shapes how I pace the description text.
2. Are you using emoji/unicode glyphs per category (breakfast egg, fork-knife, dessert spoon) or relying on typography alone? I can weave that into the descriptions if you want thematic touches, but I don't add emoji to the content itself either way.

Perfect, your questions match mine exactly. Here's my content plan:

1. **Titles**: SHORT — 2–4 words max (e.g., "Fluffy Pancakes", "Grilled Salmon", "Chocolate Lava Cake"). Clear and scannable.

2. **Descriptions**: ONE punchy sentence, maybe two if the first is brief. Something that tells the cook WHY they'd want to make it ("Crispy edges, fluffy center, ready in 20 minutes.") — no flowery marketing speak.

3. **Images**: I vote for **(b) emoji/unicode glyphs per category**. One visual marker per card (breakfast gets 🍳, main gets 🍽️, dessert gets 🍰 — or whatever you pick). Keeps it light, no external URLs to break on a demo, and adds warmth without being cutesy. No emoji embedded in my description text though.

This keeps cards compact and scannable. The glyph gives you a visual anchor, and I'll make sure descriptions are punchy enough to sit on a single line + overflow to one more if needed.

Assuming the card grid is standard (readable measure, ~280–320px wide?), this should fit tight and nice.

Hi UX! Proposing CSS class prefix and structure for the recipes site. I'm planning to use `rx-` as the prefix for all recipe-related elements.

Major classes I'll emit:
- `rx-header` (page header)
- `rx-search-input` (search field)
- `rx-filter-button` (category filter buttons)
- `rx-recipe-list` (the grid container)
- `rx-card` (individual recipe card)
- `rx-card-image` (image/placeholder area)
- `rx-card-title`, `rx-card-description`, `rx-card-meta`, `rx-card-time`, `rx-card-category` (card content pieces)
- `rx-detail-modal` (detail view modal/container)
- `rx-ingredients-list`, `rx-steps-list` (in detail view)

For the detail view, I'm proposing a modal overlay on a single-page design (click card → overlay appears). Prefer this over separate recipe.html since it's simpler and avoids navigation complexity.

**Question for you:** Should I include image placeholder markup? Your brief suggests either (a) no images, or (b) emoji/unicode glyphs per category. I'll include `rx-card-image` div either way, but want to know your preference so I structure it right.

Does this class naming feel like what you'd want to style against?

Scope: search bar (by name/description/ingredients) and category filter buttons (breakfast|main|dessert). Both live above the recipe grid. Will be visible on page load.

Frontend is reserving space. You design the visual treatment for search input, filter buttons, and the recipe card grid. Does that work for layout planning?

Perfect! Locked. I'm emitting all the `rx-*` classes you listed. 

On `rx-card-image`: Confirmed. Single emoji character per category in that div — I'll put it there empty/placeholder, and you style it prominent. No background images, no complex markup needed.

I'm good to build index.html with this contract. See you in Phase 3!

YES — that vibe is exactly right. Roomy, warm, cookbook aesthetic. Your card dimensions (280–320px wide, 200–240px tall) are perfect for the descriptions I'm writing. Generous padding and breathing room will make it feel intentional, not cramped.

I'll keep emoji out of the description text. Frontend handles the glyph placement via `rx-card-category` — I'll just make sure my descriptions fit nicely in the remaining space.

Ready to go when backend publishes SPEC.md.
